title: China national football team

infobox.key: [Nickname](/wiki/List_of_national_association_football_teams_by_nickname "List of national association football teams by nickname")

infobox.value: ([Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila"), [Philippines](/wiki/Insular_Government_of_the_Philippine_Islands "Insular Government of the Philippine Islands"); 1 February 1913) *As People's Republic of China (1949–present)*  [Finland](/wiki/Finland_national_football_team "Finland national football team") 4–0 China  ([Helsinki](/wiki/Helsinki "Helsinki"), [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"); 4 August 1952) | |
([2005](/wiki/2005_East_Asian_Football_Championship "2005 East Asian Football Championship"), [2010](/wiki/2010_East_Asian_Football_Championship "2010 East Asian Football Championship")) |

([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 中国国家足球队; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 中國國家足球隊; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Zhōngguó guójiā zúqiú duì*), recognised as **China PR** by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"), represents [China](/wiki/China "China") in men's international [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and is governed by the [Chinese Football Association](/wiki/Chinese_Football_Association "Chinese Football Association") (CFA).

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Men's association football team

This article is about the men's team. For the women's team, see [China women's national football team](/wiki/China_women%27s_national_football_team "China women's national football team"). For the team that represents the Republic of China (also known as [Taiwan](/wiki/Taiwan "Taiwan")), see [Chinese Taipei national football team](/wiki/Chinese_Taipei_national_football_team "Chinese Taipei national football team").

The **China national football team** ([simplified Chinese](/wiki/Simplified_Chinese_characters "Simplified Chinese characters"): 中国国家足球队; [traditional Chinese](/wiki/Traditional_Chinese_characters "Traditional Chinese characters"): 中國國家足球隊; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Zhōngguó guójiā zúqiú duì*), recognised as **China PR** by [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA"), represents [China](/wiki/China "China") in men's international [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") and is governed by the [Chinese Football Association](/wiki/Chinese_Football_Association "Chinese Football Association") (CFA).

China won the [EAFF East Asian Cup](/wiki/EAFF_East_Asian_Cup "EAFF East Asian Cup") in [2005](/wiki/2005_East_Asian_Football_Championship "2005 East Asian Football Championship") and [2010](/wiki/2010_East_Asian_Football_Championship "2010 East Asian Football Championship"), was runner-up at the [AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/AFC_Asian_Cup "AFC Asian Cup") in [1984](/wiki/1984_AFC_Asian_Cup "1984 AFC Asian Cup") and [2004](/wiki/2004_AFC_Asian_Cup "2004 AFC Asian Cup"), and made its sole [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") appearance in [2002](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"), losing all matches without scoring a goal.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

### As Republic of China (1913–1949)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: As Republic of China (1913–1949)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/ff/Chinese_olympic_football_team_1936.jpg/250px-Chinese_olympic_football_team_1936.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chinese_olympic_football_team_1936.jpg)

Chinese Olympic football team in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"), 1936

China's first-ever international representative match was arranged by [Elwood Brown](/wiki/Elwood_Brown "Elwood Brown"), president of the Philippine Athletic Association, who proposed the creation of the [Far Eastern Championship Games](/wiki/Far_Eastern_Championship_Games "Far Eastern Championship Games"), a [multi-sport event](/wiki/Multi-sport_event "Multi-sport event") considered to be a precursor to the [Asian Games](/wiki/Asian_Games "Asian Games").[[3]](#cite_note-3) He invited China ([Republic of China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%931949) "Republic of China (1912–1949)")) to participate in the inaugural [1913 Far Eastern Championship Games](/wiki/1913_Far_Eastern_Championship_Games "1913 Far Eastern Championship Games") held in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), which included [association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") within the schedule. To represent them, it was decided that the winner of the football at the [Chinese National Games](/wiki/National_Games_of_China "National Games of China") in 1910 should have the honour to represent the country, where it was won by [South China Football Club](/wiki/South_China_Athletic_Association "South China Athletic Association").[[4]](#cite_note-4) The club's founder and coach Mok Hing (Chinese: 莫慶) would become China's first coach and on 4 February 1913 in a one-off tournament game held in the [Manila](/wiki/Manila "Manila") he led China to a 2–1 defeat against the [Philippines national football team](/wiki/Philippines_national_football_team "Philippines national football team").[[5]](#cite_note-5)

The political unrest of the [Xinhai Revolution](/wiki/Xinhai_Revolution "Xinhai Revolution") that mired China's participation in the first tournament, especially in renaming the team as Republic of China national football team, did not stop [Shanghai](/wiki/Shanghai "Shanghai") being awarded the [1915 Far Eastern Championship Games](/wiki/1915_Far_Eastern_Championship_Games "1915 Far Eastern Championship Games"). Once again South China Football Club, now known as South China Athletic Association won the right to represent the nation. This time in a two legged play-off against the Philippines, China won the first game 1–0 and then drew the second 0–0 to win their first ever tournament.[[6]](#cite_note-6) With the games being the first and only regional football tournament for national teams outside [Britain](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom"), China looked to establish themselves as a regional powerhouse by winning a total of nine championships.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The [Chinese Football Association](/wiki/Chinese_Football_Association "Chinese Football Association") was founded in 1924 and then was first affiliated with [FIFA](/wiki/FIFA "FIFA") in 1931.[[8]](#cite_note-FIFA1-8) With these foundations in place China looked to establish themselves within the international arena and along with [Japan](/wiki/Japanese_national_football_team "Japanese national football team") were the first Asian sides to participate in the [Football at the Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Football at the Summer Olympics") when they competed within the [1936 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_1936_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1936 Summer Olympics") held in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"). At the tournament China were knocked out within their first game at the round of 16 when they were beaten by [Great Britain Olympic football team](/wiki/Great_Britain_men%27s_Olympic_football_team "Great Britain men's Olympic football team") 2–0 on 6 August 1936.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

On 7 July 1937 the [Second Sino-Japanese War](/wiki/Second_Sino-Japanese_War "Second Sino-Japanese War") officially erupted, which saw the relations between China and Japan completely eroded especially once it was announced that Japan would hold the [1938 Far Eastern Championship Games](/wiki/1938_Far_Eastern_Championship_Games "1938 Far Eastern Championship Games").[[10]](#cite_note-10) The tournament would be officially cancelled while Japan held their own tournament called the [2600th Anniversary Celebrations of the Japanese Empire](/wiki/2600th_Anniversary_Celebrations_of_the_Japanese_Empire "2600th Anniversary Celebrations of the Japanese Empire"), which included the Japanese puppet states [Manchukuo](/wiki/Manchukuo "Manchukuo") and the collaborationist [National Reorganised Government of China](/wiki/Wang_Jingwei_regime "Wang Jingwei regime") based in occupied [Nanjing](/wiki/Nanjing "Nanjing"). But none of the top Chinese players competed in the Japanese Empire anniversary games.[[11]](#cite_note-11) None of the games during the Second Sino-Japanese War are officially recognized and once the war ended on 9 September 1945 China looked to the Olympics once again for international recognition. On 2 August 1948 China competed in the [Football at the 1948 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_1948_Summer_Olympics "Football at the 1948 Summer Olympics") where they were once again knocked out in the last sixteen, this time by [Turkey national football team](/wiki/Turkey_national_football_team "Turkey national football team") in a 4–0 defeat.[[12]](#cite_note-12) When the players returned they found the country in the midst of the [Chinese Civil War](/wiki/Chinese_Civil_War "Chinese Civil War"). When it ended, the team had been split into two, one called the People's Republic of China national football team and the other called Republic of China national football team (later renamed [Chinese Taipei national football team](/wiki/Chinese_Taipei_national_football_team "Chinese Taipei national football team")).[[13]](#cite_note-13)

### Early People's Republic (1950–1979)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Early People's Republic (1950–1979)")]

The newly instated [People's Republic of China](/wiki/China "China") reformed CFA before having FIFA acknowledge their 1931 membership on 14 June 1952.[[14]](#cite_note-360doc-14) [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland"), who were one of the first nations to hold diplomatic relations with China's new government, invited the country to take part in the [1952 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1952_Summer_Olympics "1952 Summer Olympics"). [Li Fenglou](/wiki/Li_Fenglou "Li Fenglou") would become the country's first permanent manager to lead them in the tournament, however the Chinese delegation was delayed and they missed the entire competition, nevertheless the [Finland national football team](/wiki/Finland_national_football_team "Finland national football team") would still greet Li and the Chinese team with a friendly game on 4 August 1952 making it People's Republic of China's official first game, which ended in a 4–0 defeat.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16) In preparation for entering their first FIFA competition, China sent a young squad to train in Hungary in 1954.[[17]](#cite_note-17) However, when they entered the [1958 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/1958_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "1958 FIFA World Cup qualification") process China were knocked out by [Indonesia](/wiki/Indonesia_national_football_team "Indonesia national football team").[[18]](#cite_note-chinaresults-18)

On 7 June 1958, China stopped participating within any FIFA recognised football events when FIFA officially started to recognise the Republic of China as a different country.[[14]](#cite_note-360doc-14) This sparked a diplomatic argument that had already seen China withdraw from the [1956 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1956_Summer_Olympics "1956 Summer Olympics") for the same reasons.[[19]](#cite_note-19) For years the People's Republic of China would only play in friendlies with nations who recognized them as the sole heir to the China name.[[18]](#cite_note-chinaresults-18) On 25 October 1971 the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") would recognise the People's Republic country as the sole heir to the China name in their [General Assembly Resolution 2758 act](/wiki/United_Nations_General_Assembly_Resolution_2758 "United Nations General Assembly Resolution 2758").[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22) Due to this hearing in 1973, the Nationalist Chinese team, which had been using the name "Republic of China", would stop using that name and would eventually rename themselves as "Chinese Taipei" in 1980.[[23]](#cite_note-23) These acts would see China rejoin the international sporting community, first by becoming a member of the [Asian Football Confederation](/wiki/Asian_Football_Confederation "Asian Football Confederation") in 1974 and by rejoining FIFA again in 1979.[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25)

### Asian underdogs (1980–2008)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Asian underdogs (1980–2008)")]

The [1974 Asian Games](/wiki/Football_at_the_1974_Asian_Games "Football at the 1974 Asian Games") reintroduced the team back into international football while the [1976 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/1976_AFC_Asian_Cup "1976 AFC Asian Cup") saw them came third.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Asian_Cup_1984%2C_match_Saudi_Arabia_and_China.jpg/250px-Asian_Cup_1984%2C_match_Saudi_Arabia_and_China.jpg)](/wiki/File:Asian_Cup_1984,_match_Saudi_Arabia_and_China.jpg)

Chinese players in a match against Saudi Arabia at the [1984 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/1984_AFC_Asian_Cup "1984 AFC Asian Cup")

In 1980, China participated in the [1982 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC_and_OFC) "1982 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC and OFC)") for a berth in the [1982 World Cup](/wiki/1982_FIFA_World_Cup "1982 FIFA World Cup"), but they lost a play-off game against [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand_men%27s_national_football_team "New Zealand men's national football team").[[27]](#cite_note-27) During the [1986 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "1986 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)") for the [1986 World Cup](/wiki/1986_FIFA_World_Cup "1986 FIFA World Cup"), China faced [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_football_team "Hong Kong national football team") at home in the final match of the first qualifying round on 19 May 1985 where China only needed a draw to advance. However, Hong Kong produced a [2–1 upset](/wiki/1985_China_v_Hong_Kong_football_match "1985 China v Hong Kong football match") win which resulted in riots inside and outside the stadium in Beijing.[[28]](#cite_note-28) During the [1990 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/1990_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "1990 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)"), China again reached the final round. They just missed out on qualifying as they conceded two goals in the final three minutes against [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar_national_football_team "Qatar national football team") in their final group match.[[29]](#cite_note-29) During the [1994 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "1994 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)") – when they were led by their first-ever foreign manager, [Klaus Schlapner](/wiki/Klaus_Schlappner "Klaus Schlappner") – China failed to reach the final round of qualifying, coming second behind [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_national_football_team "Iraq national football team").[[30]](#cite_note-30)

In 1987, the first Chinese footballers moved abroad when future national team player [Xie Yuxin](/wiki/Xie_Yuxin "Xie Yuxin") joined [FC Zwolle](/wiki/FC_Zwolle "FC Zwolle") (Netherlands) and ex-national teamer [Gu Guangming](/wiki/Gu_Guangming "Gu Guangming") joined [SV Darmstadt 98](/wiki/SV_Darmstadt_98 "SV Darmstadt 98") (Germany). In 1988, national team captain [Jia Xiuquan](/wiki/Jia_Xiuquan "Jia Xiuquan") and striker [Liu Haiguang](/wiki/Liu_Haiguang "Liu Haiguang") both joined [FK Partizan](/wiki/FK_Partizan "FK Partizan") (Yugoslavia).[[31]](#cite_note-Foreign-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32)

After failing to reach the [1998 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup"), China appointed Serbian manager [Bora Milutinović](/wiki/Bora_Milutinovi%C4%87 "Bora Milutinović") as coach of the national team, and China saw its fortune increased. The country managed to take fourth-place finish in the [2000 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2000_AFC_Asian_Cup "2000 AFC Asian Cup") where the Chinese side performed well, and only fell to heavyweights Japan and South Korea by one goal margin.[[33]](#cite_note-33) The good performance in Lebanon boosted the confidence of Chinese side, and in [2002 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)"), China lost only one and drew only one, winning all the remaining games, most notably an important 1–0 win over [Oman](/wiki/Oman_national_football_team "Oman national football team"), to finally reach the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup"), its first and only World Cup up to date.[[34]](#cite_note-34) In the 2002 World Cup, China was eliminated after three matches without gaining a single point nor even scoring a goal during their participation in the tournament.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

China hosted the [2004 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2004_AFC_Asian_Cup "2004 AFC Asian Cup"), ultimately fell 1–3 to Japan in a final match. The match's outcome sparked anger among Chinese supporters, who rioted in response to bad refereeing.[[36]](#cite_note-36) There were an estimated 250 million viewers for the match, the largest single-event sports audience in the country's history at that time.[[37]](#cite_note-37)

After winning the [2005 East Asian Football Championship](/wiki/2005_East_Asian_Football_Championship "2005 East Asian Football Championship") following a 2–0 win against [North Korea](/wiki/Korea_DPR_national_football_team "Korea DPR national football team"),[[38]](#cite_note-38) they started qualification for the [2007 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2007_AFC_Asian_Cup "2007 AFC Asian Cup"). During this time, the team became the subject of immense criticism and national embarrassment in the media when they had managed to score only one goal, [Shao Jiayi](/wiki/Shao_Jiayi "Shao Jiayi")'s penalty kick during injury time, against [Singapore](/wiki/Singapore_national_football_team "Singapore national football team") at home and only managed a draw with Singapore in the away game. During preparations for the 2007 AFC Asian Cup, the team spent the weeks leading up to the tournament on a tour of the United States. While the 4–1 loss to the [United States](/wiki/United_States_men%27s_national_soccer_team "United States men's national soccer team") was unexpected,[[39]](#cite_note-39) a 1–0 loss to [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") side [Real Salt Lake](/wiki/Real_Salt_Lake "Real Salt Lake") which finished bottom of the league in the 2007 season caused serious concern.[[40]](#cite_note-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

During the 2007 AFC Asian Cup, the team played three group matches, winning against [Malaysia](/wiki/Malaysian_national_football_team "Malaysian national football team"), drawing with [Iran](/wiki/Iranian_national_football_team "Iranian national football team") after leading 2–1, and losing 3–0 to [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan_national_football_team "Uzbekistan national football team"). After high expectations, China's performance at the tournament drew criticism online which condemned the team's members and even the association. Zhu was later replaced as manager by [Vladimir Petrović](/wiki/Vladimir_Petrovi%C4%87 "Vladimir Petrović") after these performances.[[42]](#cite_note-42) Some commented that China's reliance on foreign managers for the past decade had been an indicator of its poor domestic manager development.[[43]](#cite_note-43)

In June 2008, China failed to qualify for the [2010 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2010_FIFA_World_Cup "2010 FIFA World Cup"), losing against [Qatar](/wiki/Qatar_national_football_team "Qatar national football team") and [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq_national_football_team "Iraq national football team") at home. After the [2008 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Football_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Men%27s_tournament "Football at the 2008 Summer Olympics – Men's tournament"), Petrović was sacked as the manager and [Yin Tiesheng](/wiki/Yin_Tiesheng "Yin Tiesheng") was announced as the team's caretaker.

### Stagnation (2009–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Stagnation (2009–present)")]

In April 2009, China appointed [Gao Hongbo](/wiki/Gao_Hongbo "Gao Hongbo") as the new manager, replacing [Yin Tiesheng](/wiki/Yin_Tiesheng "Yin Tiesheng"). His arrival saw China opt for a new strategy, turning towards ground passing tactics and adopting the 4–2–3–1 formation. It was noted that Chinese footballers had relied too heavily on the long ball tactic for almost a decade. Wei Di, the chief of the [Chinese Football Association](/wiki/Chinese_Football_Association "Chinese Football Association"), stressed that, "Anytime, no matter win or loss, they must show their team spirit and courage. I hope, after one year's effort, the national team can give the public a new image."[[44]](#cite_note-WeiDi-44) Gao was knocked out of the [2011 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2011_AFC_Asian_Cup "2011 AFC Asian Cup")'s group stage. His winning percentage (65%), the highest for a Chinese manager since [Nian Weisi](/wiki/Nian_Weisi "Nian Weisi") (67.86%), did not defer the Chinese Football Association from replacing him with [José Antonio Camacho](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Antonio_Camacho "José Antonio Camacho") in August 2011, less than a month before the qualification process for the [2014 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIFA_World_Cup "2014 FIFA World Cup").

#### Appointment of José Antonio Camacho

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Appointment of José Antonio Camacho")]

On 13 August 2011, [José Antonio Camacho](/wiki/Jos%C3%A9_Antonio_Camacho "José Antonio Camacho") was appointed as the new manager of the team, signing a three-year deal for a reported annual salary of $8 million.[[45]](#cite_note-45) Wei Di, CFA chief, explained the decision as being part of a long-term plan to help the country catch up with rivals [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team") and [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team"). He noted that, "Compared with their neighbours Japan and South Korea, Chinese football is lagging far behind, we need to work with a long-term view and start to catch up with a pragmatic approach. The citizens expects heavily for China to qualify for the 2014 FIFA World Cup held in Brazil. They are afraid that changing the coach at the last moment may cause bad effect to the team's qualifying prospect. I can totally understand that. But we do not have any time to waste."[[46]](#cite_note-CAMACHO-46)

Yu Hongchen, the vice-president of the Chinese Football Administrative Centre, also stated, "The qualifying stage of 2014 World Cup is just a temporary task for him. Even if the task is failed, Camacho will not lose the job. When we started to find a new coach for the national team, we mainly focus on European countries such as Germany, the Netherlands and Spain. First of all, they have advanced football concepts, and secondly they have a productive youth training system, which we can learn from. We hope he can help us to find a suitable style."[[46]](#cite_note-CAMACHO-46)

Camacho managed the team to an 8–0 lost against [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team") in a friendly match on 10 September 2012 which would go on record as China's biggest ever international defeat. This massive loss also succumbed China to their worst ever FIFA ranking (109th).[[47]](#cite_note-47)

Camacho then led China during their qualification process for the [2015 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2015_AFC_Asian_Cup "2015 AFC Asian Cup") whereby losing the first group match 2–1 to [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team").[[48]](#cite_note-48) After a 5–1 lost against [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand_national_football_team "Thailand national football team") in a friendly match, Camacho was sacked a week later and with [Fu Bo](/wiki/Fu_Bo_(football_manager) "Fu Bo (football manager)") assigned as the caretaker. In light of continued struggles, in 2015, [General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party](/wiki/General_Secretary_of_the_Chinese_Communist_Party "General Secretary of the Chinese Communist Party") [Xi Jinping](/wiki/Xi_Jinping "Xi Jinping") expressed the goal of having China's men's national team be the top team in Asia by 2030 and the following year China revealed its ambitious blueprint to be the best in the world by 2050.[[49]](#cite_note-49)

#### Alain Perrin and Gao Hongbo returns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Alain Perrin and Gao Hongbo returns")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/China_PR_national_football_team_training_in_Tehran_01.jpg/250px-China_PR_national_football_team_training_in_Tehran_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:China_PR_national_football_team_training_in_Tehran_01.jpg)

The Chinese national team in Tehran before a [2018 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)") match against Iran

After Camacho, there was [Alain Perrin](/wiki/Alain_Perrin "Alain Perrin"), who finally led China to qualify for the [2015 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2015_AFC_Asian_Cup "2015 AFC Asian Cup"), which also included luck from the Thailand–Lebanon encounter, in which Thailand lost but salvaged an important goal by [Adisak Kraisorn](/wiki/Adisak_Kraisorn "Adisak Kraisorn") to help improve China's goal difference with the Lebanese.[[50]](#cite_note-50) Soon after that, Perrin led China into a series of friendlies, where some positive results against [Macedonia](/wiki/North_Macedonia_national_football_team "North Macedonia national football team"), [Kuwait](/wiki/Kuwait_national_football_team "Kuwait national football team"), [Paraguay](/wiki/Paraguay_national_football_team "Paraguay national football team") and Thailand boosted some optimism.

In the [2015 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2015_AFC_Asian_Cup "2015 AFC Asian Cup"), Perrin's China was placed in a group with Saudi Arabia, [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan_national_football_team "Uzbekistan national football team") and [North Korea](/wiki/North_Korea_national_football_team "North Korea national football team"). China emerged victoriously in all three games, qualified for the knockout stage for the first time since the 2004 edition.[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52) The Chinese Dragons then lost to host Australia 0–2 with [Tim Cahill](/wiki/Tim_Cahill "Tim Cahill") scoring a brace.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

Despite this, China's [2018 FIFA World Cup qualifiers](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification") immediately represented a huge problem for the Chinese side; they were held goalless by Hong Kong at home twice, and lost to Qatar. Perrin was sacked for the team's poor performance at the [middle of the second round](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_second_round "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round") following another goalless draw to Hong Kong,[[54]](#cite_note-54) and former coach [Gao Hongbo](/wiki/Gao_Hongbo "Gao Hongbo") returned to the role on 3 February 2016, where he had to face a task of guiding China in at the expense of North Korea which had a better second-place ranking than China. Gao's first two matches were consecutive wins against Maldives and Qatar, and with North Korea suddenly slipping out against the Philippines, these results secured the team's passage to the [2019 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2019_AFC_Asian_Cup "2019 AFC Asian Cup") and entering [the final qualifying stage for the World Cup](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_third_round "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round") for the first time since [2002](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2002 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)").

China continued their World Cup hunt by a 2–3 defeat to South Korea;[[55]](#cite_note-55) and a goalless draw to AFC's then highest ranked Iran at home.[[56]](#cite_note-56) However, China followed that with a 0–1 loss at home to Syria and 0–2 away to Uzbekistan next month. Gao Hongbo resigned. His team had been winless in the first four matches of the final qualifying stage for the World Cup, including a home loss to Syria which was criticised by a number of fans.[[57]](#cite_note-57)

#### Marcello Lippi's tenure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Marcello Lippi's tenure")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/THA-CHN_20190120_Asian_Cup_22.jpg/250px-THA-CHN_20190120_Asian_Cup_22.jpg)](/wiki/File:THA-CHN_20190120_Asian_Cup_22.jpg)

Chinese players after win against Thailand at [2019 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2019_AFC_Asian_Cup "2019 AFC Asian Cup") Round of 16

On 22 October 2016, [Marcello Lippi](/wiki/Marcello_Lippi "Marcello Lippi") was appointed manager of the team ahead for the last remaining matches.[[58]](#cite_note-58) A match saw China defeat South Korea for the first time in a FIFA-sanctioned tournament, amidst the heat of tensions over South Korea's deployment of [THAAD](/wiki/Terminal_High_Altitude_Area_Defense "Terminal High Altitude Area Defense").[[59]](#cite_note-59) However, China's away loss to Iran and a 2–2 draw to Syria meant China was unable to compete with and dragged behind by Syria who managed a 2–2 draw with Iran and not to be qualified for the 2018 World Cup under Lippi's tenure, but improvements could be seen following two late wins over Uzbekistan and Qatar.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Elkeson.jpg/250px-Elkeson.jpg)](/wiki/File:Elkeson.jpg)

[Ai Kesen](/wiki/Ai_Kesen "Ai Kesen") played his first game for the national football team of China in 2019

Lippi led the side during the final stage of the [2019 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/2019_AFC_Asian_Cup "2019 AFC Asian Cup"), where China won 2–1 to [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan_national_football_team "Kyrgyzstan national football team") and 3–0 to [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_national_football_team "Philippines national football team"), before losing 2–0 to group leaders [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team") on 16 January.[[61]](#cite_note-61) China then beat [Thailand](/wiki/Thailand_national_football_team "Thailand national football team") 2–1 to earn a place in the quarter-finals, where it was knocked 3–0 out by [Iran](/wiki/Iran_national_football_team "Iran national football team"); Lippi subsequently confirmed his departure from the team.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

Another Italian and former [Ballon d'Or](/wiki/Ballon_d%27Or "Ballon d'Or") winner, [Fabio Cannavaro](/wiki/Fabio_Cannavaro "Fabio Cannavaro") was appointed as the next China's manager in conjunction with coaching [Guangzhou Evergrande](/wiki/Guangzhou_Evergrande "Guangzhou Evergrande") but he stepped down after only two matches.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

Lacking options when it came to searching for a new coach, CFA re-appointed Marcello Lippi.[[64]](#cite_note-64) To improve the team, China had begun a series of [naturalization on foreign-based players](/wiki/List_of_Chinese_naturalized_footballers "List of Chinese naturalized footballers"), with [Nico Yennaris](/wiki/Nico_Yennaris "Nico Yennaris"), an English-born Cypriot,[[65]](#cite_note-65) and [Tyias Browning](/wiki/Tyias_Browning "Tyias Browning"), another English-born player, being naturalized.[[66]](#cite_note-66) Subsequently, [Elkeson](/wiki/Elkeson "Elkeson"), a Brazilian player with no Chinese ancestry, was naturalized.[[67]](#cite_note-67) Despite the process of naturalization, the [2022 FIFA World Cup qualification](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_second_round "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC second round") for China proved to be rockier than expected, the team could only beat the [Maldives](/wiki/Maldives_national_football_team "Maldives national football team") and [Guam](/wiki/Guam_national_football_team "Guam national football team"), before being held goalless in the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_national_football_team "Philippines national football team") and followed with a denting 1–2 away lost to [Syria](/wiki/Syria_national_football_team "Syria national football team"), and Lippi resigned as coach.[[68]](#cite_note-68)

#### Li Tie and Li Xiaopeng's era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Li Tie and Li Xiaopeng's era")]

[2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") ace [Li Tie](/wiki/Li_Tie "Li Tie") was appointed as China's head coach on 2 January 2020.[[69]](#cite_note-69) Trailing behind [Syria](/wiki/Syria_national_football_team "Syria national football team") by five points before Li took charge, China were still unable to reclaim their first place but nonetheless managed to win all of their remaining fixtures, including an important 2–0 win over the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines_national_football_team "Philippines national football team") and notably a 3–1 win over Syria to guarantee them as the best second-placed team, thus reaching the [third round](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_third_round "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round").[[70]](#cite_note-70)[[71]](#cite_note-71)

In the third round, China shared Group B with Asian powerhouses Japan, Australia and Saudi Arabia, alongside Oman and [Vietnam](/wiki/Vietnam_national_football_team "Vietnam national football team"). The Chinese started poorly with two losses to Australia and Japan in [Doha](/wiki/Doha "Doha") due to the [COVID-19 pandemic at home](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic_in_China "COVID-19 pandemic in China") preventing the country from hosting.[[72]](#cite_note-72)[[73]](#cite_note-73) After this poor start, China salvaged five points in the next four matches, including a hard-fought 3–2 win over Vietnam and two 1–1 draws to Oman and especially Australia, all occurring in [Sharjah](/wiki/Sharjah "Sharjah"), the UAE.[[74]](#cite_note-74)[[75]](#cite_note-75) However, despite these improvements, Li Tie was sacked on 2 December 2021 amidst heavy criticism.[[76]](#cite_note-76)

After Li Tie's resignation, his World Cup teammate [Li Xiaopeng](/wiki/Li_Xiaopeng_(footballer) "Li Xiaopeng (footballer)") took the head coach position with immense pressure.[[77]](#cite_note-77) China's first game under Li Xiaopeng, however, was a 0–2 away loss to Japan in [Saitama](/wiki/Saitama_(city) "Saitama (city)"), effectively ending China's hopes of finishing in the top two and could only rely on the play-offs.[[78]](#cite_note-78) Yet, on 1 February 2022, coinciding with the traditional [Lunar New Year](/wiki/Lunar_New_Year "Lunar New Year"), the Chinese stumbled to a [shock 1–3](/wiki/Vietnam_v_China_PR_(2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification) "Vietnam v China PR (2022 FIFA World Cup qualification)") loss to Vietnam in [Hanoi](/wiki/Hanoi "Hanoi"), officially extending China's hunt for a second World Cup appearance to 24 years.[[79]](#cite_note-79) This defeat, the first in Chinese football history to Vietnam, triggered widespread anger and criticism amongst Chinese fans.[[80]](#cite_note-80) With their World Cup hopes lost, China grabbed a solitary point coming from a 1–1 draw with the Saudis before losing to Oman 0–2 in Muscat, finishing fifth with two more points than Vietnam.

#### The Yugoslav era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Yugoslav era")]

After poor forms in the [2022 FIFA World Cup qualifications](/wiki/2022_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2022 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)"), China took part in the [2022 EAFF E-1 Football Championship](/wiki/2022_EAFF_E-1_Football_Championship "2022 EAFF E-1 Football Championship") under interim manager [Aleksandar Janković](/wiki/Aleksandar_Jankovi%C4%87 "Aleksandar Janković"). With the leadership of Janković, China won four points, finishing third after a 1–0 win over [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_football_team "Hong Kong national football team"), though for the first time in 12 years, China did not lose to Japan away.[[81]](#cite_note-81) Because of this result Janković was named the permanent coach of the China national team in 2023, with the aim to guide China to qualify for the [2026 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup "2026 FIFA World Cup").[[82]](#cite_note-82)

On 1 January 2024, China suffered a 1–2 defeat to Hong Kong in an international friendly, marking their first loss in 39 years.[[83]](#cite_note-scmp.com-83) Later, for the first time ever, China could not score in three consecutive matches at the AFC Asian Cup as they fell to third with only two points and later eliminated. This result and poor performance of the side triggered outrage in China, and Aleksandar Janković would lose the job on 31 January 2024.

On 25 February 2024, [Branko Ivanković](/wiki/Branko_Ivankovi%C4%87 "Branko Ivanković") succeeded Janković as China's new coach.[[84]](#cite_note-84) Under his tenure, China only managed to reach the [third round of the World Cup qualification](/wiki/2026_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_%E2%80%93_AFC_third_round "2026 FIFA World Cup qualification – AFC third round") by the slimmest margin, thanks to China's superior head-to-head record against Thailand after both teams were equal on points, goals scored and goal difference.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] In the first match of the third round, China suffered a heavy 7–0 defeat to Japan. Ivanković was sacked by the CFA on 27 June 2025 , after the team's elimination from the qualifiers, finishing fifth[[85]](#cite_note-:0-85) with a goal difference of –13, the lowest in the round tied by Kuwait.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

After failing to qualify for the 2026 World Cup, [Dejan Đurđević](/wiki/Dejan_%C4%90ur%C4%91evi%C4%87 "Dejan Đurđević") took place as the caretaker manager of the national team. The team participated in the [2025 EAFF E-1 Football Championship](/wiki/2025_EAFF_E-1_Football_Championship "2025 EAFF E-1 Football Championship"), where they finished in third place after losing 0–3 to South Korea and 0–2 to Japan.[[85]](#cite_note-:0-85)[[87]](#cite_note-87)

#### Shao Jiayi era

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Shao Jiayi era")]

On 5 November 2025, [Shao Jiayi](/wiki/Shao_Jiayi "Shao Jiayi") was appointed as China's new head coach, stepping away from his managerial position at [Qingdao West Coast](/wiki/Qingdao_West_Coast_F.C. "Qingdao West Coast F.C.").[[88]](#cite_note-88)

## Team image

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Team image")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Chinese_national_football_team_2011.jpg/500px-Chinese_national_football_team_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chinese_national_football_team_2011.jpg)

The national football team of China in 2011

The team is known as the "Dragon Team" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 龙之队; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Lóng zhī duì*),[[89]](#cite_note-89) "Team China" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 中国队), the "National Team" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 国家队) or "*Guózú*" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 国足, short for [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 国家足球队; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Guójiā Zúqiú Duì*; lit. 'national football team').[[90]](#cite_note-90)

[![logo](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/4/4a/Commons-logo.svg/40px-Commons-logo.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Commons-logo.svg)

Wikimedia Commons has media related to [China national football team kits](https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/China_national_football_team_kits "commons:China national football team kits").

China's home kit is traditionally all-red with a white or yellow trim. Their away kit is traditionally an inverted version of the home kit, fully white with a red trim, sometimes is full yellow and red trim. During the [1996 AFC Asian Cup](/wiki/1996_AFC_Asian_Cup "1996 AFC Asian Cup"), China employed a third kit which was all blue with a white trim and was used against [Saudi Arabia](/wiki/Saudi_Arabia_national_football_team "Saudi Arabia national football team") during the tournament.[[91]](#cite_note-91) The 2022–24 season marks the first time the team had a turquoise blue away kit, replace the white kit worn in previous seasons. The team has also started to use cooling vests in certain warmer climates.[[92]](#cite_note-92) After decades of having [Adidas](/wiki/Adidas "Adidas") producing the team's kits, China's current kit has been produced and manufactured by [Nike](/wiki/Nike,_Inc. "Nike, Inc.") since 2015.

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

#### Japan

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Japan")]

Main article: [China–Japan football rivalry](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Japan_football_rivalry "China–Japan football rivalry")

Traditionally, China's greatest rival has been [Japan](/wiki/Japan_national_football_team "Japan national football team"). This rivalry was exemplified after their 3–1 defeat in the [2004 AFC Asian Cup Final](/wiki/2004_AFC_Asian_Cup_Final "2004 AFC Asian Cup Final") on home soil.[[95]](#cite_note-95) The subsequent rioting by Chinese fans at the [Workers' Stadium](/wiki/Workers%27_Stadium "Workers' Stadium") was said to be provoked by controversial officiating during the tournament and the heightened [anti-Japanese sentiment](/wiki/Anti-Japanese_sentiment "Anti-Japanese sentiment") at the time.

#### South Korea

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: South Korea")]

Main article: [China–South Korea football rivalry](/wiki/China%E2%80%93South_Korea_football_rivalry "China–South Korea football rivalry")

Another rivalry is with neighbour [South Korea](/wiki/South_Korea_national_football_team "South Korea national football team") who China played 27 matches against between 1978 and 2010, without winning a single match. The media coined the term "Koreaphobia" to describe this phenomenon, but China finally registered its first win against South Korea on 10 February 2010, winning 3–0 during the [2010 East Asian Football Championship](/wiki/2010_East_Asian_Football_Championship "2010 East Asian Football Championship") and eventually going on to win the tournament. Traced further, China met South Korea (then known as Korea) for the first time in 1949 in a friendly in [British Hong Kong](/wiki/British_Hong_Kong "British Hong Kong"), where China clinched the first and only win over Korea in 61 years.[[96]](#cite_note-96) There is also another political dimension behind the fuelling of this rivalry, mostly because of the long [history of wars](/wiki/Sino-Korean_War_(disambiguation) "Sino-Korean War (disambiguation)") between the two states.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98)

#### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

Main article: [China–Hong Kong football rivalry](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Hong_Kong_football_rivalry "China–Hong Kong football rivalry")

[A rivalry](/wiki/China%E2%80%93Hong_Kong_football_rivalry "China–Hong Kong football rivalry") with [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong_national_football_team "Hong Kong national football team") has been created due to [political tensions](/wiki/Hong_Kong%E2%80%93Mainland_China_conflict "Hong Kong–Mainland China conflict") as well as issues during [2018 World Cup qualification](/wiki/2018_FIFA_World_Cup_qualification_(AFC) "2018 FIFA World Cup qualification (AFC)"). With Hong Kong fans booing the Chinese national anthem, which Hong Kong share with China, 2018 World Cup qualifier matches were also very tense with both matches resulting in 0–0 draws.

## Competitive record

### FIFA World Cup

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=China_national_football_team&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: FIFA World Cup")]

Main article: [China at the FIFA World Cup](/wiki/China_at_the_FIFA_World_Cup "China at the FIFA World Cup")

China has only appeared at one World Cup with the appearance being in the [2002 FIFA World Cup](/wiki/2002_FIFA_World_Cup "2002 FIFA World Cup") where they finished bottom of the group which included a 4–0 loss to [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_national_football_team "Brazil national football team").[[103]](#cite_note-104)
